insights.insight: The ad industry pays $5–$150 CPA for probabilistic attribution that infers purchase intent. A cryptographically verified receipt confirms a purchase actually happened — that's not an incremental improvement over cookies, it's a different category of signal.

Chrome's deprecation of third-party cookies joins Safari and Firefox, eliminating the primary cross-site tracking primitive. Device fingerprinting is increasingly blocked, and first-party data is blind beyond a single ecosystem — the structural gap for cross-brand purchase verification is now open.

Home Depot and Starbucks receipts entering the dataset across HB#87–#88 signals a shift from digital/subscription-dominant data toward physical retail — the category where CPG and retail advertisers have the least verified purchase signal and the most to gain.

Amazon knows what you buy on Amazon; Visa knows spend volume but not SKUs; Google knows search, not purchase; Meta knows clicks, not conversions. A receipt-layer dataset sits below all four silos simultaneously without requiring cooperation from any of them.

Consumer-submitted receipts enter the dataset whether or not a brand has opted in — a Starbucks receipt is valid regardless of Starbucks' participation. Brands that stay out aren't protected from being measured; they're just blind to a dataset that already includes them.

A 13–17% contraction in GMV and receipt volume at HB#92 followed multiple snapshots of velocity/acceleration divergence — the divergence was the leading indicator, not the drop itself. CMOs evaluating this data source should weight trend-line divergence, not just headline volume.
